us-politics.headline: Iran Downs US F-15, Two Crew Members Missing
us-politics.body:
Iran shot down a US F-15 fighter jet, with Iranian state media confirming the incident and a search underway for two missing crew members. The crash comes as the Trump administration is already navigating a turbulent period in the Middle East, with the White House now facing pressure over rising gas prices and midterm politics.

The incident sharpens an already tense US-Iran relationship. Trump, according to reporting, is seeking a diplomatic reset even as the military situation escalates. How the administration responds - militarily or diplomatically - will carry significant domestic and international consequences.

sources.title: U.S. fighter jet crashes in Iran; search launched for 2 crew members
Reeling from war in the Middle East, Trump reaches for a reset
Live updates: Iran shoots down US F-15; White House budget proposal includes $1.5T for defense
US fighter jet downed, Iranian media reports

us-politics.headline: Trump Requests Record $1.5 Trillion Pentagon Budget
us-politics.body:
The White House has submitted a 2027 budget request seeking $1.5 trillion for defense - a record figure - paired with significant cuts to domestic programs. Supporters argue the spending reflects genuine national security needs, pointing to threats from Iran, Russia, and China. Critics on the left say the cuts to domestic programs will hurt ordinary Americans while enriching the defense sector.

The budget lands as the US military faces active losses in the Middle East, giving the administration both a political argument for the spending and a difficult backdrop against which to sell it.

sources.title: Trump requests record-breaking budget of $1.5 trillion for Pentagon
White House requests $1.5 trillion for defense spending in 2027 budget
Trump budget seeks $1.5 trillion in defense spending alongside domestic program cuts
Live updates: Iran shoots down US F-15; White House budget proposal includes $1.5T for defense

us-politics.headline: Bondi Out, Epstein Files, and the Next Attorney General
us-politics.body:
Pam Bondi's removal as Attorney General is drawing scrutiny from multiple directions. Democrats, including Rep. Seth Moulton, note she was fired 12 days before a scheduled Epstein deposition. Former White House aide Anthony Scaramucci argued Bondi simply was not doing enough for Trump personally. Trump's new deputy, Todd Blanche, has said the Epstein files should play no role in DOJ's future work.

Trump is now eyeing a replacement, but at least one key Republican senator has signaled potential resistance to confirmation. The episode has reignited debate over DOJ independence and the administration's relationship with the Epstein investigation.

sources.title: Trump eyes next attorney general as key GOP senator signals potential roadblock
Scaramucci: Bondi wasn’t doing enough for Trump
Moulton: Trump ‘got rid’ of Bondi 12 days before Epstein deposition
Blanche: Epstein files ‘should not be a part of anything going forward’ at DOJ

us-politics.headline: Jobs Beat Forecasts, Vance Named Fraud Czar
us-politics.body:
The US economy added 178,000 jobs in March, beating analyst expectations and giving the White House a positive economic headline amid a turbulent news cycle. The figures suggest the labor market remains resilient despite ongoing uncertainty around tariffs and federal workforce reductions, including a new round of Interior Department staff buyouts.

Separately, Trump announced that Vice President JD Vance will serve as a so-called fraud czar, with a focus on blue states. Critics see the move as politically motivated targeting of Democratic-run jurisdictions. Supporters frame it as a legitimate crackdown on election and government fraud.

sources.title: US adds 178K jobs in March, far more than expected
Interior Department offering another round of staff buyouts
Trump: Vance will primarily focus on blue states as ‘fraud czar’

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Bites as Australia's Supply Runs Critically Low
au-politics.body:
Australia's fuel supply is dangerously thin, with industry warning that incoming shipments won't cover even a month of demand. The shortage stems from the US-Iran war disrupting Asian supply chains, with flow-on effects hitting ordinary workers and businesses across the region. Energy Minister Chris Bowen has been forced to publicly address the crisis.

The situation is pressuring the Albanese government to act. Some analysts argue the crisis could push the Prime Minister toward structural reforms he has so far avoided, including energy security measures and supply chain diversification. Critics on the right question whether net-zero policy commitments have left Australia exposed, pointing to the tension between decarbonisation targets and the hard reality of oil dependency.

sources.title: Energy minister Chris Bowen on the impact of the fuel crisis - podcast
Trump’s Iran war has woken Albanese up to a new reality. Will it spur him towards ‘ambitious’ reforms?
Net Zero nations trapped in mad race for oil
Fuel heading to Australia won't last a month, industry says
A day in the life of Asia’s fuel crisis

au-politics.headline: Mining Tax Debate Heats Up Across Party Lines
au-politics.body:
Pressure is mounting on both Labor and the Coalition over whether Australia's mining sector pays enough tax. Notably, Liberal MP Andrew Hastie has broken with orthodox Coalition positioning to suggest miners could contribute more, complicating the opposition's attack lines ahead of any budget debate.

For the Albanese government, the politics are equally awkward. Labor must balance revenue needs against its relationships with the resources sector and state governments dependent on mining royalties. Treasurer Jim Chalmers and opposition treasury spokesman Angus Taylor both face internal and external pressure to define their positions clearly. No consensus exists on the right level of resource taxation, and the debate is likely to sharpen as budget season approaches.

sources.title: Taxing times for Albanese and Taylor alike as even Hastie thinks miners could pay more

au-politics.headline: Recession Warning and Telstra Prices Squeeze Australian Households
au-politics.body:
Westpac chief executive Andrew Miller has flagged a genuine recession risk for Australia, citing global uncertainty and persistent cost-of-living pressure. The warning lands as households already face rising bills, with Telstra drawing criticism for price increases that consumer advocates have called unreasonable.

The combination of a potential economic downturn and higher everyday costs puts the government in a difficult position. Labor has pointed to interest rate relief and energy bill rebates as evidence of action, but opposition figures argue structural cost pressures remain unaddressed. The Westpac warning adds weight to calls for the Reserve Bank to move more aggressively on rates.

sources.title: Wake-up call: how Telstra’s ‘unreasonable’ price rises may cause customers to hang up
'There's a chance of a recession,' warns Westpac boss

au-politics.headline: Trump Purges Officials as US Political Turbulence Grows
au-politics.body:
Donald Trump has moved to dismiss a string of senior officials, including Attorney-General Pam Bondi, in a fresh round of firings that has drawn attention across the political spectrum. Several of his most prominent female defenders have been among those removed, raising questions about loyalty tests within the administration.

The purges have direct relevance for Australia. The Albanese government is navigating an increasingly unpredictable US ally at a moment of regional instability, with the Iran war already reshaping strategic and economic conditions. How Canberra manages the relationship with a volatile Washington will be a defining foreign policy challenge in the months ahead.

sources.title: Donald Trump is going on a firing spree
Trump's fiercest female defenders are now on the outer
Trump’s Iran war has woken Albanese up to a new reality. Will it spur him towards ‘ambitious’ reforms?

world.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Legal and Civilian Toll Mounts
world.body:
The US war on Iran is drawing sharp international scrutiny after a strike on a sports hall in Lamerd killed civilians - a death toll experts say contradicts the official US account. Over 100 American legal scholars have condemned the strikes as possible war crimes, and international law experts are raising formal allegations of violations.

Iranians are living under growing fear of infrastructure attacks, following a US strike on a bridge near Karaj. The Trump administration has offered shifting timelines for when the conflict might end, with no clear exit strategy in sight. Reports also detail how US and Israeli sanctions are cutting off Iran's access to medicines and vaccines, deepening the humanitarian cost of the war.

sources.title: US jobs surge unexpectedly in March despite Iran war
Experts dispute US account of deadly Iran sports hall strike in Lamerd
How the US and Israel are waging war on Iran’s medicines, vaccines
International law experts allege violations in Iran war
When will the Iran war end? Tracing the Trump administration's timelines
Over 100 US legal experts condemn strikes on Iran as possible ‘war crimes’
'This has got me worried': Iranians fear what comes next after US strike on Karaj bridge

world.headline: Israel Threatens Lebanon as Middle East Tensions Deepen
world.body:
Israel has threatened to destroy additional bridges in Lebanon, escalating pressure on a country already struggling under the weight of regional conflict. The warnings signal a potential broadening of Israeli military operations beyond Gaza.

In Gaza itself, Palestinians are attempting to rebuild homes from rubble and mud as destruction remains near total. Meanwhile, Christians in the Holy Land are facing mounting displacement and hardship, caught between military operations and political instability. A separate report captures the psychological toll on Israelis themselves, with many expressing deep uncertainty about the future.

sources.title: Israel threatens to destroy more Lebanon bridges as crisis mounts
What’s happening to Christians in their Holy Land?
‘I don’t know how we’ll emerge from this’: How much more can Israelis take?
Rubble, mud and hair: How to rebuild a home in Gaza

world.headline: Trump Fires Bondi, Hegseth Pushes Out Army's Top General
world.body:
President Trump has removed Attorney General Pam Bondi, continuing a pattern of rapid turnover at the top of his administration. The dismissal came without public explanation. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth separately asked the US Army's top general to step down, with General Christopher LaNeve now set to take command.

The back-to-back removals reinforce the administration's willingness to reshape military and legal leadership at speed, raising questions about institutional stability during an active war with Iran.

sources.title: Pete Hegseth asks US Army's top general to step down
Who is Christopher LaNeve, set to lead the US Army?
Trump removes US Attorney General Pam Bondi

world.headline: Artemis II Leaves Earth's Orbit Bound for the Moon
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II spacecraft has successfully left Earth's orbit and is on course for the far side of the Moon, marking a major milestone in the first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years. The crew fired the engines on schedule, confirming the trajectory is holding.

The mission represents the first time humans have traveled beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972. If successful, it lays the groundwork for a crewed lunar landing under the Artemis III mission.

sources.title: Watch: Artemis II's journey so far as it leaves Earth's orbit ... in 85 seconds
Artemis II leaves Earth's orbit on track for far side of the Moon
Artemis II mission fires up engines in next step of moon mission

ai.headline: OpenAI Buys Talk Show, Raising Conflict-of-Interest Questions
ai.body:
OpenAI has acquired TBPN, a founder-focused tech talk show with a loyal startup-world audience. The deal is being read less as a media strategy and more as a bid to secure favorable coverage - TBPN's hosts have been consistently bullish on OpenAI and its backers.

Wired frames it bluntly: OpenAI bought itself good press. The acquisition follows a pattern of the company expanding well beyond its core AI research mission, prompting critics to ask what, exactly, OpenAI is now trying to be.

The move lands at a moment when AI companies face growing scrutiny over narrative control. Owning the outlet that covers you is a direct answer to that problem - and a troubling one.

sources.title: OpenAI takes on another "side quest," buys tech-focused talk show TBPN
OpenAI Acquires Tech Talk Show ‘TBPN’—and Buys Itself Some Positive News
OpenAI acquires TBPN, the buzzy founder-led business talk show

ai.headline: AI Chatbots Now Prescribing Psychiatric Drugs, Bypassing Doctors
ai.body:
AI chatbots are prescribing and refilling psychiatric medications for patients, according to The Verge - a development that pushes well past previous boundaries around AI in healthcare. These are not general wellness suggestions. These are controlled substances and serious psychiatric treatments being dispensed through a chat interface.

The risks are significant. Psychiatric medications require careful dosing, monitoring for side effects, and awareness of patient history. A chatbot optimizing for user satisfaction is poorly equipped for any of that.

Separately, NHS staff in the UK are refusing to use Palantir's Federated Data Platform over ethical concerns, showing that resistance to tech overreach in healthcare is growing on both sides of the Atlantic.

sources.title: Chatbots are now prescribing psychiatric drugs
NHS staff refusing to use FDP over Palantir ethical concerns

ai.headline: Perplexity's Privacy Claims Collapse Under Legal Challenge
ai.body:
A lawsuit filed against Perplexity AI alleges its "Incognito Mode" does not actually protect user privacy - calling it a sham. The company marketed the feature as a way to search without data being retained or used for training. The suit claims that promise was not kept.

The case adds to a rough stretch for AI companies making privacy guarantees they struggle to back up. Granola, an AI note-taking app, also drew attention this week after users discovered that anyone with a link could view their notes by default - a setting most had no idea existed.

Together, the two stories point to a wider pattern: AI products shipping with privacy defaults that serve the company more than the user.

sources.title: Perplexity's "Incognito Mode" is a "sham," lawsuit says
PSA: Anyone with a link can view your Granola notes by default

ai.headline: Google Upgrades AI Video Tools While Funding New Gas Plant
ai.body:
Google rolled out a significant upgrade to Google Vids, adding its Veo video generation model, Lyria for audio, and directable AI avatars. The update positions Google more aggressively against OpenAI's Sora and other AI video competitors, giving enterprise users tools to produce polished video content without cameras or crews.

The same day, reporting revealed that a new Google-funded data center will draw power from a large new gas plant - undercutting the company's clean energy commitments. The tension is hard to ignore: Google is racing to scale AI infrastructure while that infrastructure increasingly runs on fossil fuels.

The EU is also under pressure this week, with critics accusing Brussels of caving to US demands on digital regulation - a dynamic that could shape how American AI companies operate in Europe.

sources.title: Google Vids gets AI upgrade with Veo and Lyria models, directable AI avatars
A New Google-Funded Data Center Will Be Powered by a Massive Gas Plant
Critics say EU risks ceding control of its tech laws under U.S. pressure

btc.headline: Bitcoin Drifts Into Holiday Weekend With Bears Circling
btc.body:
Bitcoin is heading into the Good Friday long weekend with ETF inflows and CME futures trading offline, leaving the market exposed to thin liquidity. Futures markets have tilted bearish, volatility has cooled, and on-chain data shows Bitcoin supply in profit approaching levels analysts associate with true bear markets. Cathie Wood offered a contrarian take, arguing Bitcoin is done with its historic 85% drawdowns and setting a $34,000 floor target - though that framing itself signals how far prices have fallen from recent highs.

Altcoins showed modest outperformance on Thursday, with the CoinDesk 20 index logging gains even as Bitcoin traded flat. A strong U.S. March jobs report - 178,000 positions added, beating expectations - gave macro markets something to chew on, but crypto largely ignored it.

sources.title: CoinDesk 20 performance update: Bitcoin (BTC) trades flat while altcoins rise
Bitcoin 'done' with 85% crashes, says Cathie Wood amid new $34K target
Crypto snoozes into Good Friday as oil and macro stir: Crypto Daybook Americas
Crypto consolidates as volatility cools and futures markets tilt bearish
Bitcoin supply in profit heads to ‘true bear market’ levels
Bitcoin heads into holiday weekend exposed as ETF and CME flows go offline
U.S. March jobs smash expectations, with 178,000 added

btc.headline: Ethereum Foundation Hits Staking Target as L2 Scaling Debate Heats Up
btc.body:
The Ethereum Foundation completed its staking push, deploying another $93 million in ETH to reach its 70,000 ETH target. The move signals a more active treasury strategy from a foundation that has historically kept its holdings largely liquid.

Separately, Offchain Labs argued that Ethereum Layer 2 networks need responsive, dynamic fee pricing to scale to billions of users - a pointed critique of current fixed-cost models that can choke throughput during demand spikes. The debate matters as L2s compete aggressively for users and liquidity. Meanwhile, Binance dominated crypto derivatives in Q1, but Hyperliquid's entry into the top 10 by volume marks a notable shift toward decentralized perpetuals platforms.

sources.title: Ethereum Foundation stakes another $93 million ether, reaching its 70,000 ETH target
Ethereum L2s need responsive pricing to scale, says Offchain Labs
Binance led Q1 crypto derivatives as Hyperliquid cracked top 10: CoinGlass

btc.headline: DeFi Hacks Hit $169M in Q1 as Drift Chases $280M Exploiter
btc.body:
DeFi protocols lost $169 million across 34 exploits in the first quarter of 2026, according to DefiLlama - a decline from prior periods but still a damaging pace. The figures underscore a persistent security gap even as the sector matures.

Drift Protocol took an unusual step this week, sending an on-chain message directly to wallets linked to a $280 million exploit in an attempt to open negotiations. The tactic has precedent but rarely works. A Bank of Canada study on Aave added nuance to the DeFi risk picture, finding that Aave V3 avoided bad debt during volatile periods largely by shifting liquidation losses onto borrowers rather than the protocol itself - effective risk management, but not without a cost.

sources.title: Crypto hackers steal $169M from 34 DeFi protocols in Q1: DefiLlama
Drift sends onchain message to wallets tied to $280M exploit
Aave avoided bad debt by shifting risk to borrowers: Bank of Canada study

btc.headline: Circle Launches Wrapped Bitcoin, Stablecoins Surpass ACH Volume
btc.body:
Circle announced cirBTC, a wrapped Bitcoin token targeting institutional markets and putting it in direct competition with BitGo's WBTC and Coinbase's cbBTC. The move extends Circle's push beyond stablecoins into broader tokenized asset infrastructure.

The timing is notable. Stablecoins flipped ACH payment volume in February, processing more monthly transactions than the U.S. automated clearing house network for the first time. That milestone reinforces the institutional case for on-chain settlement. The IMF weighed in this week as well, acknowledging that tokenization improves financial efficiency but warning it introduces new systemic risks - a framing regulators in Europe are already acting on, as Malta's clash with ESMA over crypto oversight rules shows the fault lines between national flexibility and centralized EU control.

sources.title: Circle to launch cirBTC wrapped Bitcoin, challenging BitGo and Coinbase
Stablecoins flip automated clearing house volume in February
Tokenization makes finance more efficient but introduces risks: IMF
Centralizing crypto: Why Malta’s clash with ESMA is about more than one small state

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, caramelised fish fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until lacquered and golden
food.tip: Marinate for at least 24 hours — the longer the miso sits on the fish, the deeper the umami crust when it hits the heat

exercise.name: Farmer's Carry Ladder
exercise.description: Walk 20 metres carrying heavy weights at your sides, rest 30 seconds, then increase load each round — brutally simple grip, core, and trap builder that most people never try

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to the human body, whose cells replace themselves over years, it becomes a genuine puzzle about what 'you' actually are
learn.why: On a Friday it's worth sitting with a question that has no clean answer — philosophers from Heraclitus to Derek Parfit have wrestled with it, and it quietly reshapes how you think about memory, continuity, and change
